sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Fluorescein staining is used to identify corneal epithelial defects such as abrasions, foreign body tracks, ulcers, or dendritic lesions. In prehospital care it should be used selectively, mainly when the result will clarify eye management and when a penetrating injury has been reasonably excluded.

sections.title: Indications
sections.content:
- Suspected corneal abrasion (following trauma, foreign body, or contact lens-related injury)
- Evaluation of corneal foreign body - to identify epithelial tracking or residual defect after removal
- Suspected corneal ulceration
- Chemical or UV (arc eye / photokeratitis) exposure
- Any red eye with pain and/or photophobia where corneal integrity is uncertain

sections.title: Contraindications & Precautions
sections.content:
- Known allergy to sodium fluorescein is rare but should be checked
- Soft contact lenses should be removed before application because fluorescein can stain them
- Do not use if penetrating injury, globe rupture, major deformity, or prolapsed ocular contents are suspected
- If the patient has severe pain, reduced vision, marked photophobia, or chemical exposure, definitive eye priorities may be irrigation, protection, and transport rather than staining

sections.title: Preparation
sections.content:
- Gather fluorescein strips or drops, saline if required, cobalt blue light, gloves, and gauze or tissues
- Explain the examination and obtain consent where the patient has capacity
- Remove contact lenses if present and safe to remove
- Protect the eye and proceed to transport rather than staining if penetrating injury or globe rupture is a concern

sections.title: Procedure
sections.content:
1. Position the patient comfortably with the head supported

2. If using a strip, moisten it lightly with saline; if using drops, prepare the dropper without contaminating the tip

3. Ask the patient to look up and apply the dye to the lower conjunctival sac or lower fornix without touching the cornea with force

4. Ask the patient to blink to distribute the dye

5. Examine the cornea and visible conjunctiva with cobalt blue illumination, darkening the environment if practical

6. Look for focal green staining, foreign body tracks, ulceration, or a dendritic pattern

7. Document the affected eye, approximate location of staining, and any associated red-flag findings such as reduced vision or severe pain

sections.title: Post-Procedure & Notes
sections.content:
- Reduced vision, suspected ulcer, contact-lens-related keratitis, dendritic lesions, or significant pain should prompt urgent escalation
- Fluorescein does not rule out serious ocular pathology outside the corneal surface
- Do not provide topical local anaesthetic for repeated self-administration after the assessment
- Document whether staining changed management or simply supported the need for eye protection and transport
